planting.note: Direct-sow seeds or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun in spring; queen's crepe myrtle requires consistently warm temperatures to flower — it blooms most reliably in zone 10–12 with a dry period in winter followed by the rainy season, which triggers flower bud initiation; in zone 9 it rarely flowers satisfactorily and is better replaced by the common crepe myrtle (L. indica).

directions: Direct-sow seeds or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun; queen's crepe myrtle (giant crepe myrtle, banaba) is a large tropical tree native to South and Southeast Asia that is the largest and most spectacular flowering member of the crepe myrtle family — it can grow to 40–60 feet in ideal tropical conditions and in summer and early monsoon season produces enormous, terminal panicles (12–18 inches long) of large, lavender-pink to rose-purple, crinkled, crepe-textured flowers in the characteristic crepe myrtle style, but at a scale dwarfing the common crepe myrtle (Lagerstroemia indica). The bark exfoliates in patches like all crepe myrtles, revealing a smooth, gray-beige inner bark surface that is ornamental year-round. The leaves turn brilliant red-orange in the dry season before falling. Banaba leaf extract has been intensively researched for its anti-diabetic properties and is widely sold as an herbal supplement, particularly in the Philippines, where the tree is called banaba. An outstanding flowering shade tree for zone 10–12 tropical and subtropical landscapes.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun in spring; African tulip tree grows very rapidly — often reaching 20 feet within a few years of planting — and begins producing flowers within 3–5 years from seed. It is considered invasive in Hawaii, parts of Florida, and many Pacific Island nations where it has escaped cultivation and displaced native vegetation in disturbed forest areas.

directions: Direct-sow seeds or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun; African tulip tree (fountain tree, fireball) is a large, fast-growing West African tropical tree of the bignonia family (Bignoniaceae) that produces one of the most spectacular and instantly recognizable flower displays of any tropical tree — large, upright, cup-shaped, terminal clusters of 10–20 large, flared, bright scarlet-orange to crimson flowers with yellow-edged petals are produced at the branch tips year-round in zone 10–12 tropical climates, creating a constant flaming-red display from a distance. The flower buds hold water — a trait used by children in the native range who squeeze the buds to squirt water — and are visited by sunbirds and bats as pollinators. The tree grows rapidly to 40–50 feet with a dense, spreading canopy, making it an effective but large shade tree. It has become invasive in Hawaii, Pacific island nations, and parts of tropical Florida, where it spreads aggressively by wind-borne seeds into disturbed forest areas — check local regulations before planting.

planting.note: Scarify or nick seeds before sowing to improve germination, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun in spring; yellow flame tree is fast-growing, drought-tolerant once established, and highly adaptable to different soil types including coastal sands — it is one of the most successful street trees in tropical cities throughout Southeast Asia and the Caribbean.

directions: Scarify seeds before direct-sowing or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in full sun; yellow flame tree (copper pod, copperpod) is a large tropical tree native to Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia and widely planted throughout the tropical world as a street, shade, and ornamental tree of exceptional quality — it produces dense, spreading canopy of finely divided, bipinnate, feathery, dark-green leaves that create a graceful, filtered shade, and from summer through fall, produces large, upright, terminal and lateral panicles of vivid yellow, five-petaled, fragrant flowers that bloom in flushes and can blanket the canopy in golden yellow. After the flowers, the tree produces masses of distinctive, flat, winged pods that mature through russet-brown to bright copper-red, persisting ornamentally on the tree for months and giving it the common name 'copper pod'. The tree's fine foliage, yellow flowers, copper seedpod clusters, and attractive form make it ornamental across multiple seasons. It is fast-growing, drought-tolerant once established, and widely used as a street tree in tropical cities across Southeast Asia, the Caribbean, and South Florida.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun in spring; pink trumpet tree (formerly Tabebuia rosea) flowers most reliably when it experiences a distinct dry season in winter — the moisture stress and cool temperatures of a dry winter followed by the onset of rain and warmth in spring triggers the spectacular leafless flowering display.

directions: Direct-sow seeds or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; pink trumpet tree (formerly Tabebuia rosea, now Handroanthus roseus) is a large tropical deciduous tree native to Central America and northern South America that produces one of the most breathtaking spring flowering displays in the tropical world — in spring (late dry season), before or as the leaves emerge, the entire leafless tree becomes covered in large, funnel-shaped, lavender-pink to pale-purple-pink, trumpet-form flowers borne in dense, rounded clusters at every branch tip, transforming the canopy into a billowing cloud of soft-pink that is visible from great distances. The flowering display lasts 2–4 weeks, with the fallen pink petals carpeting the ground beneath the tree in a second display. The broad, palmate, compound, five-leaflet leaves are attractively tropical during the growing season. Pink trumpet tree is widely planted as a street and park tree throughout Central America, the Caribbean, and South Florida, where it is one of the most dramatic ornamental trees available for tropical zone 10–12 landscapes.

planting.note: Propagate from suckers (offsets from the base) or plant container-grown specimens in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, fertile soil in full sun after frost; traveler's palm grows from the center of a single, expanding fan and should be given ample space for the spread to develop symmetrically — planting in windy areas without protection will tear the large paddle-shaped leaves and destroy the ornamental symmetry of the fan.

directions: Propagate from suckers or plant container specimens in moist to well-drained, humus-rich, fertile soil in full sun; traveler's palm is a Malagasy giant of the bird-of-paradise family (Strelitziaceae) — not a true palm — that creates one of the most architecturally dramatic plant forms in the tropical landscape: an enormous, perfectly flat, two-dimensional fan of enormous, paddle-shaped, banana-like leaves aligned precisely in one plane, held on long, smooth, gray-green leaf sheaths that arch outward from the base and, as the plant matures, from an increasingly tall trunk. The fan alignment was traditionally said to run roughly east-west, allowing travelers to orient themselves — giving the plant its common name. The leaf sheaths hold reservoirs of fresh water at their bases. In established plants, small, white, bird-of-paradise-like flowers emerge from boat-shaped bracts along the upper stem. The plant produces basal offshoots (suckers) that can be separated to propagate new specimens. Traveler's palm is one of the most iconic and unmistakable specimens of the tropical landscape, widely planted throughout zone 10–12 botanical gardens, resorts, and private estates.
